summary: The tendency to attribute human thoughts, feelings, or intentions to animals, objects, or systems.
The tendency to assume other people are more similar to you than they really are.
The tendency to answer a hard question by unconsciously replacing it with an easier one.
The tendency for candidates listed first on a ballot to receive a small voting advantage.
The tendency to like someone more after doing that person a favor.
The tendency to remember a scene as having included more surrounding space than was actually shown.

name: Compromise effect
summary: The tendency for an option to seem better when it appears as a middle compromise between extremes.
The tendency to remember past attitudes or behavior as more consistent with the present than they really were.

name: Regressive bias
summary: The tendency to remember high values as lower and low values as higher, pulling estimates back toward the middle.
The tendency to mistake an old memory or borrowed idea for a new original thought.
The tendency for informed people to underestimate how hard it is for less-informed people to follow or predict the same material.
The tendency for a worse third option to shift preference toward a nearby target option.
The tendency to ignore quantity or scope when judging a total outcome.
The tendency to remember imagined, suggested, or reconstructed events as if they had actually happened.
The tendency to remember information better when you generated it yourself.
The tendency to remember humorous material more easily than plain material.
The tendency to give disproportionate weight to immediate payoffs over larger later ones.
The tendency to think you understand other people better than they understand you.
The tendency for automatic associations to shape response speed and judgment even without deliberate endorsement.
The tendency to doubt one's competence and fear being exposed as a fraud despite evidence of ability.
The tendency for information processed more deeply to be remembered better.
The tendency to feel losses more strongly than equivalent gains.
The tendency to treat effortful learning as ineffective even when it produces stronger long-term retention.
The tendency for the last items in a sequence to be remembered better when heard than when read.
The tendency to treat a prior good deed as permission for later worse behavior.
The tendency to expect other people to be more self-interested or biased than they really are.
The tendency for recall to worsen while waiting for your turn to speak because attention shifts to performance.
The tendency for a researcher's expectations to influence how data are observed, elicited, or interpreted.
The tendency for some disadvantaged groups to rate outgroups more favorably than their own group.
The tendency to see members of other groups as more similar to one another than members of your own group.
The tendency for being shown some items from a list to make the remaining items harder to recall.
The tendency to judge an experience mainly by its peak and its ending rather than by its full duration.
The tendency to treat prevention spending as buying more safety than equally effective detection or response spending.
The tendency to remember the first items in a sequence especially well.
The tendency for slightly harder-to-process material to be remembered better because it receives more effortful attention.
The tendency to judge rhyming statements as more truthful or convincing.
The tendency to remember information better when it relates to yourself.
The tendency to remember the beginning and end of a sequence better than the middle.
The tendency to remember information better when repetitions are spread out over time.

name: Stereotype memory bias
summary: The tendency for memory to drift toward familiar racial, gender, or social stereotypes.
The tendency to assign group-based traits to an individual without enough individual evidence.
The tendency to accept statements as true when they fit your beliefs, hopes, or impressions.
The tendency for questions or cues from others to shape what is later remembered as your own memory.
The tendency for time to seem to speed up or slow down during acute stress or danger.
The tendency to remember the gist of what was said better than the exact wording.
The tendency to remember an item better when it stands out from the rest.
The tendency to remember unfinished or interrupted tasks better than completed ones.
The tendency to see gains for one side as requiring losses for another even when both could benefit.
